title: Jittery Quantum Boomerang Effect
abstract: We study the dynamics of a spin-polarized wave packet in a disordered Rashba two-dimensional electron gas and identify a jittery quantum boomerang effect in which longitudinal and transverse motion return to the origin through fundamentally distinct mechanisms. Starting from an initial state with finite momentum along $x$ and spin polarized along $z$, we calculate the time evolution by combining a Chebyshev expansion of the time-evolution operator with a disorder ensemble average. In the weak-scattering regime, equations of motion derived from the quantum kinetic equation reproduce the numerical trends and show that impurity scattering acts as a viscous damping mechanism that suppresses the transient Zitterbewegung and drives the transverse displacement back to $y=0$ at long times. In contrast, the longitudinal dynamics show a Drude-like saturation at weak disorder. These results are consistent with the vanishing intrinsic spin Hall conductivity in the disordered Rashba model and with experimental observations of a transient intrinsic spin Hall effect in the time-domain. As disorder increases, the longitudinal dynamics evolve to a partial return toward the origin, which signals a transition from weak antilocalization to Anderson localization in 2D.
summary: This paper analyzes the time evolution of spin-polarized electrons in a disordered Rashba 2DEG to reveal a 'jittery quantum boomerang effect.' The authors show that longitudinal motion transitions from WAL behavior toward Anderson localization, while transverse motion is forced back to zero by symmetry constraints. This work bridges concepts from SOC physics, disorder effects, and non-equilibrium dynamics.
main_problem: The study investigates the real-time dynamics of a spin-polarized wave packet in a disordered Rashba two-dimensional electron gas to identify a 'jittery quantum boomerang effect.'
main_result: The system exhibits distinct longitudinal and transverse return mechanisms: longitudinal motion shows a crossover from Weak Antilocalization (WAL) to Anderson localization, while transverse motion returns to zero due to symmetry constraints.
method: The analysis combines numerical simulations using Chebyshev expansion with analytical derivations based on the quantum kinetic equation in both weak and strong disorder regimes.
model_or_system: The model is a spin-polarized wave packet propagating in a disordered Rashba 2DEG, governed by a Hamiltonian including Rashba SOC and random on-site potentials. The dynamics are tracked via expectation values of position, momentum, and spin polarization.
key_observables: Time evolution of displacements ($\langle x(t) angle$, $\langle y(t) angle$), momentum ($\langle k_x(t) angle$), and spin components ($\langle \sigma_{x,y,z}(t) angle$).
important_parameters: Disorder strength ($W$), scattering time ($	au$), SOC strength ($\alpha$), and the weak-scattering limit.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on asymptotic constraints derived from Anderson localization for strong disorder, and approximations like the quasi-particle picture in the weak-disorder regime.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate k-space probability density evolution across different scattering regimes (weak vs. strong) and show time traces of displacements ($\langle x(t) angle$, $\langle y(t) angle$) demonstrating the boomerang effect.
paper_structure: The paper develops equations of motion for position, momentum, and spin in the weak-disorder limit using the quantum kinetic equation. It then analyzes the asymptotic behavior under strong disorder constraints to explain the full 'boomerang' reversal mechanism.
why_interesting: The study provides a detailed real-time picture of transport phenomena (WAL $	o$ localization) driven by SOC and disorder, offering insights into how symmetry dictates long-term quantum dynamics in mesoscopic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Band structure of the clean Rashba 2DEG, showing the spin-orbit-split bands. The black dots illustrate the initial condition |k0, ↑⟩= (|k0, +⟩+|k0, −⟩)/ √

Figure 3. Numerical jittery quantum boomerang dynam- ics for ensemble-averaged trajectories at different disorder strengths W, color coded by their corresponding values of τ. (a) Longitudinal displacement ⟨x(t)⟩showing the marginal quantum boomerang effect. For weak disorder, ⟨x(t)⟩shows a Drude-like relaxation approaching ⟨x(∞)⟩= ℏk0τ/m (dashed colored lines), consistent with WAL, whereas for strong disor- der it shows a partial boomerang-like U-turn toward the ori- gin, compatible with Anderson localization. (b) Transverse displacement ⟨y(t)⟩showing transient Zitterbewegung that vanishes at long times t ≫τ. (c) Trajectories from panel (a), with the axes scaled by the mean free path ℓ≈v0τ...

Figure 2. Ensemble-averaged k space time evolution of |ψ(k, t)|2, for illustrative times t in each row. The color scale for |ψ(k, t)|2 is logarithmic and normalized by the max- imum value at each time. Data on the left and right panels correspond to the weak- and strong-scattering regimes, with τ = 14.35 ps and τ = 0.25 ps, respectively. On the top panels (t = 0), the dashed circles indicate the constant energy con- tours from Fig. 1(b). On the top right panel, the inset zooms into the initial packet.

Figure 4. (a,b) Ensemble-averaged dynamics of momentum ⟨kx(t)⟩and ⟨ky(t)⟩, and (c-e) spin ⟨σx(t)⟩, ⟨σy(t)⟩, and ⟨σz(t)⟩. Data for different disorder strengths W are color coded and identified by their values of τ. The transition from weak to strong disorder is seen in the disappearance of oscillations in ⟨ky(t)⟩and in the negative dip of ⟨kx(t)⟩, which leads to the partial quantum boomerang effect observed in ⟨x(t)⟩in Fig. 3. The spin dynamics show similar behavior, with oscil- lations vanishing for strong disorder. Both ⟨kx(t)⟩and ⟨ky(t)⟩ show exponential relaxation at long times as disorder scatter- ing isotropizes the momentum distribution. However, only ⟨ky(t)⟩reaches zero...
